sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Electrical injuries occur when electrical current passes through the body, causing tissue damage. Injuries are often classified as high voltage (>1,000 volts) or low voltage (<1,000 volts). Severity depends on voltage, current pathway, duration of contact, and associated trauma. Injuries may result from direct contact, electrical arc, lightning, or flame/flash burns. Alternating current is often more hazardous than direct current because it can cause sustained muscle contraction and arrhythmias.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Pain
- Burns, entry and exit wounds
- Respiratory arrest or dysfunction
- Cardiac arrest, arrhythmias, palpitations, myocardial damage
- Urinary symptoms
- Neurological deficits, ALOC, seizures, amnesia, motor dysfunction, dysphasia, spinal cord damage
- Fractures – consider spinal injuries
- Compartment syndrome (more likely from high voltage exposure)
- Visual and auditory disturbances

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Scene safety: confirm the source is de-energised before approaching. Avoid water, downed wires, and unstable structures
- Patients may still be in contact with live current
- High-voltage injury increases the risk of deep tissue damage, compartment syndrome, cardiac arrest, and occult trauma
- Transthoracic current path increases the risk of cardiac involvement
- Delayed arrhythmias are uncommon after low-voltage exposure with a normal assessment, but cardiac monitoring is important after high-voltage injury, loss of consciousness, chest symptoms, or ECG abnormality
- Lightning strike may cause immediate cardiac and respiratory arrest
- Associated trauma from falls or blast effects should be actively sought
- Tetanus status should be reviewed where relevant

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Responder safety is paramount. Do not approach until the source is confirmed de-energised
- Primary Survey with c-spine consideration where indicated
- Consider oxygen and ventilatory support if required
- Assess and treat burns as per burns guideline
- Perform Vital Sign Survey
- Secondary / CNS survey
- Remove clothing where necessary and assess for burns and any entry and/or exit wounds
- Assess for fractures, dislocations, or muscle injury

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Apply and assess 12 LEAD ECG
- Manage arrhythmia accordingly
- If in cardiac arrest, follow that guideline
- Consider analgesia
- Consider antiemetic
- Consider IV access and fluids where indicated

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- AC, and high DC currents can cause muscular spasm/contraction in which the individual is unable to let go of the electrical source, further, exposure can cause the individual to be hurled away from the electrical source. Household electrical sources are AC
- Lightning injuries: May present with asystole and fixed pupils; aggressive resuscitation is warranted as survival rates are higher than other causes of cardiac arrest
